textInfo.title: Increased Total Thigh Muscle Fat Infiltration
textInfo.medicalDescription: Total Muscle Fat Infiltration (MFI) of the thighs is 9.459950000000003%, which is above the reference range of 4.07 - 7.79%.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The total muscle fat infiltration (MFI) in your thighs is 9.46%, which is above the normal range of 4.07% to 7.79%. This indicates a higher than average amount of fat within your overall thigh muscle tissue.

textInfo.title: Reduced Left Frontal Lobe Gray Matter Volume
textInfo.medicalDescription: The normalized gray matter volume of the left frontal lobe (6.32%) is below the normal range of [6.43% - 7.86%]. The value is marked as being outside the norm in the source document.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The size of the gray matter in the left frontal part of your brain, when adjusted for your total head size, is slightly lower than the typical range for your age and gender. The frontal lobes are important for functions like planning, decision-making, and personality.

textInfo.title: Normal Spine Finding
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI scan of the spine showed no abnormalities. The radiologist examined the vertebrae (the bones of your spine), the intervertebral discs (the cushions between the bones), the spinal cord, and the surrounding soft tissues, and found them all to be regular in appearance for your age. This means there were no signs of significant issues such as disc herniation, spinal stenosis (narrowing of the spinal canal), fractures, or other concerning changes.

textInfo.title: Normal Head Blood Vessels
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The MRI angiography of the blood vessels in your head was normal. This specific type of MRI, called a Time-of-Flight (TOF) scan, is designed to visualize blood flow. The examination showed that your arteries have a normal course and width. There were no signs of blockages, aneurysms (bulges in the vessel wall), or other vascular abnormalities.

textInfo.title: Liver Hemangioma (14 mm)
textInfo.medicalDescription: Hämangiome in Segment VII mit 14 mm
textInfo.originalDescription: Hämangiome in Segment VII mit 14 mm
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: Your MRI scan revealed a 14 mm spot in your liver, specifically in an area called segment VII. The radiologist identified this as a hemangioma, which is a common, non-cancerous growth made of a tangle of blood vessels. Hemangiomas are the most frequent type of benign liver lesion and are typically found by chance on imaging studies. They do not turn into cancer and usually do not cause any symptoms or require treatment, even at this size.

textInfo.title: Slightly Increased Hippocampus Volume
textInfo.medicalDescription: The normalized total hippocampus volume is 0.52%, which is slightly above the normal range of 0.40% - 0.51% for the patient's age and gender.
textInfo.detailedLaymanDescription: The size of your hippocampus, a brain region important for memory, was measured and compared to the total volume of your brain. It is slightly larger than the typical range for individuals of your age and sex. This is a minor finding and its clinical significance can be discussed with your doctor.
